splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions must be finalized for calculating employer withdrawal liability under ERISA. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of updated information in calculating withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be fixed by the end of the prior plan year (the statutory measurement date), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the supervised release period does not run while the person is a fugitive.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a doctrine without explicit congressional authorization.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress intended when bringing successive collateral attacks on their convictions. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from raising any claim they previously presented, in addition to Section 2255(h)'s separate requirements. If it does not apply, prisoners need only satisfy 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar does not apply to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, so prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The resolution affects how easily candidates can challenge rules on mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices nationwide, with implications for whether such disputes must be resolved post-election.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge state election laws pre-election based on competitive injury from allegedly unlawful vote-counting procedures.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates lack Article III standing to challenge state election laws pre-election because the alleged injury is too speculative before votes are actually counted.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should apply the rule of lenity or defer to agency/Sentencing Commission interpretations when criminal sentencing provisions are ambiguous. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit held deference trumps lenity. The issue has become more salient after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability without congressional authorization.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes priority over the rule of lenity, allowing deference to an anti-defendant reading of ambiguous sentencing provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision. This determines whether defendants must meet all three criminal-history criteria or just one to be disqualified from safety-valve relief from mandatory minimums, significantly affecting sentencing outcomes depending on the circuit.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is disqualifying, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, attempt, aiding and abetting) in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Sentencing Commission commentary can expand the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses not mentioned in the guideline text. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders, significantly increasing their sentencing range.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a permissible interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight, so inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether non-retroactive changes in law can serve as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others allow consideration of non-retroactive changes as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to allow this, but some circuits have found the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered as one factor among others in determining whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state criminalizes a substance not listed on federal schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the substance to be federally scheduled, or whether any offense under state law suffices. This affects career-offender and other enhancement determinations for defendants with state drug convictions.

positions.label: State law controls — no federal-schedule limitation
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' broad reference to 'an offense under state law' means any state drug conviction qualifies regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled, and there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Federal schedule required
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under federal law, requiring a match between state and federal drug schedules.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate deadline applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is a civil or criminal matter for purposes of determining the applicable appeal deadline. The majority view treats it as a civil matter with a longer deadline, while at least one circuit applies the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and therefore the civil appellate deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case and the shorter criminal appellate deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the Chapter 13 filing and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. This directly affects whether a Chapter 7 trustee can sell appreciated property to satisfy creditors after a good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the broad scope of 'property of the estate' under § 541(a) means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, not the debtor.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A), post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors who convert in good faith, consistent with the statute's structure and legislative history.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to the conditions or execution of their confinement (as opposed to its fact or duration) through habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241, or whether they may pursue such claims through other civil actions such as Bivens.
splits.description: Circuits have developed divergent approaches to the boundary between habeas corpus and civil rights remedies for prisoners challenging conditions of confinement, including BOP decisions about earned time credits and prerelease custody under the First Step Act. This longstanding split affects what procedural vehicle prisoners must use and what exhaustion requirements apply.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for challenges affecting duration
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy whenever a prisoner's claim effectively challenges the fact or duration of confinement, including challenges to BOP time-credit or prerelease custody decisions.

positions.label: Conditions claims may proceed outside habeas
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to the conditions or terms of confinement — as distinguished from its fact or duration — may be brought through civil rights actions and are not limited to habeas corpus.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly punish a defendant for the underlying violation of supervised release conditions when revoking supervised release, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether sentencing courts at revocation hearings may impose sentences that punish the defendant for the conduct underlying the violation, or whether the sentence must be justified solely by the breach of the court's trust inherent in violating supervised release conditions. The Sixth Circuit allows express punishment for violations, while other circuits limit the rationale to breach of trust.

positions.label: Courts may punish for the violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release conditions, not merely for breaching the court's trust.

positions.label: Revocation must be based on breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that the sanction at revocation must be based on the breach of the court's trust, not punishment for the underlying conduct, because the purpose of supervised release revocation is different from initial sentencing.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether denial of a motion to seal medical records qualifies for immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate review, while other circuits have taken narrower views of the collateral order doctrine's application to sealing disputes.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost without immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal, even those involving sensitive information, are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because they can be adequately reviewed on appeal from final judgment.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re City of New York

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination in the operation of places of public accommodation under Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standards for proving discrimination claims under Title II, including what constitutes differential treatment and what remedies are available. The Supreme Court granted cert to resolve the disagreement, particularly regarding the Second Circuit's approach.

positions.label: Broader standard for proving discrimination
positions.summary: Applies a more plaintiff-friendly standard for proving discrimination in places of public accommodation, requiring less direct evidence of discriminatory intent.

positions.label: Narrower standard for proving discrimination
positions.summary: Applies a more restrictive standard requiring stronger evidence of discriminatory intent in public accommodation discrimination claims.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker arranges transportation that results in injury. The issue affects liability exposure for the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims relate to the broker's services.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting motor carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are generally applicable safety regulations, not laws related to broker services.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether legal errors or unfairness in a conviction — the type of claims typically raised in habeas petitions under Section 2255 — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar such consideration, while others permit it in certain circumstances.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider claims related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a substitute for habeas relief and courts may not consider legal challenges to the validity of a conviction as extraordinary and compelling reasons.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
